name: London System
description: A solid system with d4, Bf4, and e3. Easy to learn and play.

moves.commentary: The queen's pawn controls the center and opens the bishop diagonal.

children.commentary: Black stakes a claim in the center with d5.
Nf3 develops the knight and supports the center.
Black develops the knight to a natural square.
Bf4 is the London bishop. It avoids the pin from Bg5 and stays flexible.
e6 supports the center and prepares to develop the bishop.
e3 solidifies the center and opens the bishop's diagonal.
Bd6 challenges the London bishop. White typically retreats to g3.
Bg3 keeps the bishop active and avoids the exchange.

defenses.name: King's Indian Setup
defenses.profile: Black adopts a King's Indian style setup against the London.

defenses.name: Dutch Setup
defenses.profile: The Dutch setup challenges the center with f5.

strategy.keyIdea: Build a solid, flexible pawn structure with d4, Nf3, and Bf4 that is difficult to attack and works against almost any Black setup.
strategy.middleGamePlan: After completing development, look for e4 if the center allows it, or maneuver pieces to optimal squares slowly. The bishop on f4 is key — keep it active. Rooks belong on e1 and d1 to support potential central breaks. The London is about accumulating small advantages rather than launching direct attacks.
strategy.watchOut: Playing too passively and allowing Black to seize the initiative — the London is solid but you still need a plan, not just waiting moves.
typicalGoals: Establish d4 and Bf4 as the backbone of the position
Look for an e4 break when the center allows it
Use the h2-b8 diagonal if you trade the light-squared bishop

quiz.question: You're playing the London System and your opponent plays ...c5 to challenge your d4 pawn. What's the right reaction?

options.explanation: Exchanging on d4 relieves Black's tension and opens the c-file for Black's rooks. In the London, maintaining d4 is usually preferred.

options.text: Advance e3 and hold d4 firmly
options.explanation: Correct. Holding d4 with e3 keeps your structure intact. The London's strength is its solidity — avoid unnecessary pawn trades that open the position.

options.text: Play c4 immediately to fight for the center
options.explanation: While c4 is sometimes played in the London, jumping to it immediately can undermine your solid setup. e3 first to consolidate is more consistent.

default.opponentMoveSummary: Your opponent stepped off the main theory line with an unusual move, choosing their own path rather than the expected response.
default.whyBookBreaks: The standard line assumes a particular pawn or piece configuration that this move bypasses.
default.opponentStrategy: Your opponent is trying to reach a less-charted position where their preparation or intuition gives them an edge.
forwardGuidance: Prioritize completing your development — get all minor pieces off the back rank.
Control the center with pawns or piece pressure before launching any flank operations.

f5.opponentMoveSummary: Black played f5, staking a claim on the kingside and avoiding the symmetrical d5-Nf6-e6 structure.
f5.whyBookBreaks: The London expects Black to play d5, Nf6, and e6. f5 creates an unbalanced Dutch-London hybrid structure.
f5.opponentStrategy: Dutch-London hybrid — Black plays aggressively on the kingside, aiming to disrupt White's solid setup and create dynamic play.
forwardGuidance: Develop your pieces actively; Black's f5 weakens the pawn structure and gives you targets.
Consider e3 and Nf3 to control the center; Black's kingside expansion can be exploited.

c5.opponentMoveSummary: Black played c5, attacking the center immediately instead of the standard d5 setup.
c5.whyBookBreaks: The London expects Black to play d5, Nf6, and e6. c5 signals a Benoni-style counterattack in the center.
c5.opponentStrategy: Benoni-London setup — Black counter-attacks in the center immediately, aiming for an unbalanced, asymmetrical game.
forwardGuidance: Play d5 or c6 to control the center; don't let Black's c5 dictate the structure.
Complete development before committing — the position can become sharp.

g6.opponentMoveSummary: Black played g6, fianchettoing the bishop instead of the standard d5-e6 setup.
g6.whyBookBreaks: The London expects Black to play d5, Nf6, and e6. g6 chooses a King's Indian–style fianchetto.
g6.opponentStrategy: King's Indian setup vs London — Black develops the bishop to g7 and aims for dynamic counterplay on the kingside.
forwardGuidance: Develop your pieces and control the center; Black's fianchetto can lead to a closed, maneuvering game.
Avoid overextending on the kingside; Black's g7 bishop can become powerful.
